question: What is the motive force for natural circulation in NuScale?

nuscale_ch01_0056.title: Critical Heat Flux Testing - Preliminary Fuel Design
nuscale_ch01_0056.summary: This section outlines the verification and confirmation testing programs for unique NuScale Power Module design features, focusing on areas lacking prior data or operational experience. Specifically, it describes critical heat flux (CHF) testing for the preliminary fuel design, which differs from standard PWR fuel in assembly height and coolant flow, and provided essential data for safety analysis and development of the NSP2 CHF correlation for the final fuel design.

nuscale_ch01_0018.title: Principal Design Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0018.summary: The section outlines the principal design criteria for the NuScale plant, focusing on the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) and the overall facility process. It describes the reactor core’s heat transfer process, natural coolant circulation, steam generation, and the flow of superheated steam to the turbine and condenser, emphasizing site-specific details for cooling systems and equipment layout.

nuscale_ch01_0064.title: NuScale Test and Inspection Plans
nuscale_ch01_0064.summary: This section describes NuScale’s test and inspection plans for key reactor components, focusing on the control rod drive shafts and the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves. It summarizes successful testing of control rod operability and ECCS valve performance—including unique design features—under reactor-like conditions, confirming their functionality and safety for certification purposes.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system, which uses two independent trains of decay heat removal equipment, each capable of fully cooling the reactor. In the event of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, passive systems transfer decay heat to a large, reinforced pool (the ultimate heat sink), ensuring continued cooling through natural circulation and heat rejection to the atmosphere or external sinks.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves, including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures and in borated coolant, as well as the effectiveness of unique design features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a test article representative of the reactor recirculation valve (RRV) to ensure the valves meet safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0019.title: Operating Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0019.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates at full power using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. Its small, modular, integral pressurized water reactor design features passive safety systems, including dedicated emergency core cooling and decay heat removal, and relies on a steel containment partially immersed in water for effective passive heat removal. The NPM allows for flexible power maneuvering using control rods and soluble boron, and its components are designed for in-shop fabrication and modular assembly.

nuscale_ch01_0058.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Electrically Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0058.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuScale NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design using an electrically heated 5x5 simulated fuel bundle with AREVA HTP™/HMP™ spacer grid technology. The tests, performed at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics facility, aimed to gather CHF data, validate CHF correlations, determine mixing coefficients, and measure pressure drop characteristics under various hydraulic conditions to support the NuScale Power Module (NPM) application.

nuscale_ch01_0810.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.4 Detect and Suppress Method: Exclusion zone and buffer region methodology
nuscale_ch01_0810.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals are designed to prevent conditions that could lead to unstable flow oscillations. Instead, existing reactor trip systems act before safety limits are violated, making additional detect and suppress methods unnecessary. Backup options and specific stability-related instrumentation are also not required due to the proactive protective actions already in place.

nuscale_ch01_0811.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.5 Detect and Suppress Method Trip of reactor before SAFDL violation
nuscale_ch01_0811.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or detect-and-suppress methods for thermal hydraulic stability; instead, existing reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent violation of safety limits before flow instabilities can develop. The system partially conforms to regulatory criteria by ensuring reactor trips occur before Safety Analysis Fuel Design Limits (SAFDL) are exceeded, but it does not actively detect or suppress instabilities. Backup options and additional monitoring systems are not applicable, as the current trip system is considered sufficient.

nuscale_ch01_0059.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Fluid-Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0059.summary: The section describes thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the NuScale steam generator using an electrically-heated, fluid-heated facility at SIET in Italy. The tests, which included various operational scenarios, allowed precise control and measurement of heat flux and pressure dynamics to support design validation, code benchmarking, and regulatory inspection for safe and stable steam generator operation.

edges.description: Section A discusses testing programs that generate data to support safety analyses of unique design features, while Section B provides a general description of the plant's design, including the reactor core and hot leg riser, which are relevant to understanding the motive force for natural circulation; thus, Section A's test data supports the claims made in Section B's design description.

Section A outlines the overall testing programs developed to support the safety analysis of NuScale's unique design features, while Section B provides specific results from these tests, confirming the operability and performance of certain safety components such as ECCS valves, thereby supplying evidence for the claims made in Section A.

Section A discusses testing programs that generate data to support safety analyses of NuScale's design features, while Section B describes the natural circulation cooling system that is a subject of such analyses, connecting them through the design features being tested and analyzed.

Section A outlines the general testing programs for NuScale's unique design features, while Section B provides specific results from ECCS valve demonstration tests, with both sections connected by their focus on testing and verifying safety-related components that influence natural circulation.

Section B describes that the NuScale Power Module uses natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, which is a design feature whose verification and confirmation are addressed by the testing programs mentioned in Section A.

Section A describes the physical arrangement of the reactor core and hot leg riser, which are essential for establishing the temperature and density gradients that drive natural circulation, while Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system that relies on these gradients to remove heat from the reactor core.

Section B states that the NuScale module operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A describes the physical arrangement of the reactor core and hot leg riser, which are essential for establishing the motive force (density differences) that drives natural circulation.

Section A references and includes the content of Section B as a specific subsection (1.5.1.8 Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test), indicating that Section B is part of the broader test and inspection plans described in Section A.

Section B states that the NuScale module operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A describes the system components (such as passive condensers and decay heat removal trains) that enable and support this natural circulation process.

Section A provides detailed test results and data for the NuScale fuel design that augment and validate the preliminary testing described in Section B, thereby specifying and expanding upon the general testing framework established in Section B.

Section A provides experimental data and correlations for fuel thermal performance, while Section B describes the plant's general design and process flow, with both sections contributing information necessary to understand the thermal-hydraulic behavior relevant to natural circulation in NuScale.

Section A provides thermal-hydraulic performance and critical heat flux data for the NuScale fuel design, which is foundational for understanding the motive force for natural circulation, while Section B describes test and inspection plans for various reactor components, including ECCS valves, which are relevant to system operability but do not directly address the motive force; thus, Section A's data is a prerequisite for analyzing the motive force discussed in the analysis question, whereas Section B is tangential.

Section B describes testing programs that provide data supporting the safety analysis referenced in Section A, specifically regarding the prevention of CHF violations and thermal hydraulic stability in the NuScale design.

Section B describes the physical configuration of the NuScale reactor core and hot leg riser, which provides the necessary context for understanding the natural circulation motive force that Section A's regulatory review would evaluate.

Section B describes testing programs that provide data supporting the safety analysis, which Section A references as necessary to ensure reactor trip signals prevent violation of CHF limits, thus connecting the verification of design features to the regulatory requirement for thermal hydraulic stability.

Section B describes the physical arrangement of the reactor core and hot leg riser, which establishes the elevation and temperature differences that Section A relies on for the natural circulation motive force in the NuScale design.

Section A discusses the regulatory review responsibilities for ensuring thermal hydraulic stability and preventing SAFDL violations in NuScale, while Section B provides test results confirming the operability of key reactor components, both of which are connected through their focus on demonstrating and verifying the safety and stability of the NuScale reactor design.

Section A describes thermal-hydraulic testing of the steam generator to characterize heat transfer, while Section B outlines broader verification tests (including thermal-hydraulic aspects) that provide supporting data for the safety analysis, thus Section A's results support the claims and analyses referenced in Section B.

Section A describes the heat input mechanism used in testing the steam generator's thermal-hydraulic performance, which is directly related to the motive force for natural circulation discussed in Section B's process overview of the reactor core and hot leg riser arrangement.

Section A provides critical heat flux (CHF) test data and correlations for the NuScale fuel design, which are referenced in Section B as part of the justification that reactor trip signals prevent CHF violations and thus ensure thermal hydraulic stability without the need for exclusion zone methodology.

Section A provides critical heat flux (CHF) data and correlations for the NuScale fuel design, which are used in Section B to demonstrate that reactor trip signals prevent CHF limit violations and ensure thermal hydraulic stability as required by regulatory criteria.

Section A describes the objective and results of critical heat flux (CHF) testing for the NuScale fuel design, while Section B details the methodology of the electrically-heated steam generator performance test, with both sections connected by their focus on generating and validating thermal-hydraulic data (such as heat flux and boundary conditions) necessary to understand and model the motive force for natural circulation in NuScale.

Section A and Section B both discuss methods for detecting and suppressing thermal hydraulic instabilities in the NuScale design, with Section A noting the exclusion zone method is not used and Section B specifying that reactor trip signals are used to prevent SAFDL violations, thus Section B provides a specific implementation detail for the general approach described in Section A.

Section B describes the heat flux boundary conditions and heating zones used in steam generator testing, which provide the thermal energy that creates the temperature and density differences necessary for natural circulation—the motive force referenced in the analysis question—while Section A discusses regulatory review responsibilities but does not directly address the motive force.

Section B provides experimental data on heat flux and thermal-hydraulic behavior in the steam generator, which supports the analysis in Section A regarding the mechanisms (such as heat transfer and flow stability) that contribute to natural circulation and reactor protection in NuScale.
